blocks.statement: I can apply Coulomb's law to compute the electric force between two point charges, predict the direction (attract vs. repel), and explain why it's an INVERSE-SQUARE law (just like gravity).

blocks.whatWeKnow: From Unit 2: F_grav = G m₁m₂/r². Same inverse-square SHAPE.
blocks.connection: Today: the electric version. Same equation pattern — different constant, different 'charges' instead of masses. Both fall off as 1/r².

blocks.markdown:
## The force law between charges

Yesterday you saw charges push and pull. Today you compute HOW HARD:

**F = k · q₁ · q₂ / r²**

where k = 9 × 10⁹ N·m²/C². Compare it to Unit 2's gravity, F = G·m₁·m₂/r² — same shape, charge swapped in for mass. Both are **inverse-square laws**: double the distance, the force drops to ¼; triple it, 1/9.

Two differences from gravity matter:

- **Direction comes from the signs.** Same signs (both +, both −) → repel. Different signs → attract. Gravity only attracts.
- **k is gigantic compared to G.** Two +1 C charges 1 m apart would repel with 9 × 10⁹ N. That's why lab charges are measured in μC (10⁻⁶ C) and nC (10⁻⁹ C) — and why electric forces, not gravity, run everything at the atomic scale.

terms.definition: F = k · q₁ · q₂ / r². The electric force between two point charges. k = 9 × 10⁹ N·m²/C² (Coulomb's constant). Two +1 C charges 1 m apart would repel with F = 9 × 10⁹ N — HUGE. (That's why we usually deal with μC or nC in lab.)
terms.cognate: Sp. ley de Coulomb · Pt. lei de Coulomb · HC lwa Coulomb

terms.definition: Force falls off as 1/r². Double the distance → ¼ the force. Triple the distance → 1/9 the force. Gravity (F = Gm₁m₂/r²) and Coulomb force (F = kq₁q₂/r²) are both inverse-square. So is light intensity and sound intensity.
terms.cognate: Sp. ley del inverso del cuadrado · Pt. lei do inverso do quadrado · HC lwa envès kare

terms.definition: An idealization — a charge concentrated at one point with no size. Coulomb's law applies exactly to point charges, approximately to small charged objects. A charged balloon isn't a point — but at distances much larger than the balloon, it acts like one. Same for electrons + protons.
terms.cognate: Sp. carga puntual · Pt. carga pontual · HC chaj pwen

blocks.title: The 'square' is in the DENOMINATOR — and don't lose the sign
blocks.markdown: If r doubles, you divide F by 2² = 4. **NOT** by 2. And the SIGN of the force tells you direction: same signs (both +, both −) → repel; different signs → attract. Don't lose the sign in algebra.

blocks.instruction: Sketch two charged spheres with the force arrows on each. Show what happens when you DOUBLE the distance between them. (Inverse-square — force drops by 4.)
prompts: Two spheres, force arrows on EACH (equal and opposite).
Label the new force as F/4.

blocks.prompt: TWO EQUAL POSITIVE CHARGES — Two equal +2 μC charges are 0.30 m apart. Compute the force on each.
blocks.given: q₁ = q₂ = +2 × 10⁻⁶ C · r = 0.30 m · k = 9 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²
blocks.answer: F = 0.4 N on each charge, pushing them APART (like charges repel).

blocks.prompt: INVERSE-SQUARE SCALING — Same two +2 μC charges, but now 0.60 m apart (double the distance). What's the new force?
blocks.answer: F' = 0.1 N. Doubling distance → force divided by 4. Inverse-square.

blocks.prompt: YOUR TURN — PROTON AND ELECTRON IN A HYDROGEN ATOM. A proton (+e) and an electron (−e) are 5.3 × 10⁻¹¹ m apart (Bohr radius). Compute the magnitude of the Coulomb attraction. e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.
blocks.givenHint: q₁ = q₂ = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C (magnitudes) · r = 5.3 × 10⁻¹¹ m · k = 9 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²
blocks.equationHint: Same move as the worked example: F = kq₁q₂/r². Watch the exponents — the answer is around 10⁻⁸ N.

blocks.title: Why atoms hold together
blocks.markdown: The Coulomb attraction inside an atom is ENORMOUS at that scale — about 10⁸ N when scaled per kg of mass. That's what holds atoms together. Gravity, by contrast, is irrelevant at this distance.

blocks.prompt: Two +3 μC charges are 0.1 m apart. Compute the force. (k = 9 × 10⁹.) Show one line of work. Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: Coulomb's law and Newton's gravity both fall off as ___. The Coulomb constant k is much ___ than the gravitational constant G, so electric forces dominate at the ___ scale; gravity dominates at the ___ scale.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **32.3–32.7** (Coulomb's law; conductors and insulators; charging by friction, contact, and induction; charge polarization). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
